title: Hash collisions - when two keys want the same slot
author: Prodipta Acharjee
concept: Different keys can hash to the same slot - a collision. A common fix is chaining: each slot holds a small list, and lookup scans it. Collisions are why a hash map is O(1) on average, not always.
explanation: Back to the hash map. The hash function turns a key into a slot, but it can't give every key its own - two keys may land together. That's a collision, and it's normal, not a bug. The usual handling is chaining: each slot holds a little list of the pairs that landed there, and a lookup hashes to the slot then scans that short list. Keep collisions rare (enough slots, a good hash) and the lists stay tiny, so lookup stays effectively constant. Let them pile up and it slides toward O(n) - which is why 'O(1) on average' has that 'on average' in it.
code_example:
# "cat" and "dog" happen to hash to the same slot.
# chaining: that one slot holds a list of both pairs.
bucket = [("cat", 1), ("dog", 2)]
print(dict(bucket)["dog"])

exercises.question: What does this print?
hints: Look up the value paired with "dog".

exercises.question: Two different keys hashing to the same slot is a...
Handling collisions by keeping a list in each slot is called...
hints: The pairs form a little chain in the slot.
